description:
You are helping a junior data-scientist prepare a couple of small CSV files for a
    reproducible analysis pipeline.  
    Everything is already under the user’s home directory, but the current
    permissions are not secure enough for a multi-user environment.  
    Your job is to:

    1. LOCK the raw data so nobody can modify it.  
       • Every *.csv* file inside */home/user/datasets/raw/* must end up with the
         exact octal mode **440**  
       • The directory */home/user/datasets/raw/* itself must end up with the
         exact octal mode **550**

    2. CREATE a new sibling directory */home/user/datasets/clean/* that will hold
       cleaned versions of every CSV found in *raw/*.  
       • The directory itself must end up with the exact octal mode **750**

    3. CLEAN each CSV:  
       • Keep the header row unchanged.  
       • Remove every data row that contains at least one empty field
         (i.e. two consecutive commas `,,` or a trailing comma at end of line).  
       • Save the cleaned file in */home/user/datasets/clean/* with the
         same base-name as the original.  
       • Each cleaned CSV must end up with the exact octal mode **640**

    4. PRODUCE an audit log */home/user/clean_dataset.log* containing one line per
       cleaned file.  
       • Format **must be exact**:  
         `<filename>,<octal-permission>`  
         Example line: `data1.csv,640`  
       • List the files in **alphabetical order**.  
       • Use Unix line-endings (`\n`) and NO trailing blank line.

    IMPORTANT:
    • Do **not** change ownership (you do not have sudo).  
    • The entire job should be possible with a short series of 3–4 shell
      commands (loops & redirection allowed).  
    • The automated checker will validate directory modes, file modes,
      file contents, and the precise contents of */home/user/clean_dataset.log*.

    Hints you might think about (but the exact commands are up to you):
      – `chmod` for permissions  
      – `mkdir -p` for directory creation  
      – simple `for` loops with `awk`/`grep`/`csvtool`/`python -c` for cleaning  
      – `stat -c "%a"` to capture permissions for the log

    When you are done, you should be able to run the following quick
    self-check:

        stat -c "%a %n" /home/user/datasets/raw
        stat -c "%a %n" /home/user/datasets/raw/*.csv
        stat -c "%a %n" /home/user/datasets/clean
        stat -c "%a %n" /home/user/datasets/clean/*.csv
        cat /home/user/clean_dataset.log

    …and see that every permission and file content matches the specification above.
truth:
Initial set-up provided to the container BEFORE the agent starts:

    1. Directories  
       • /home/user/datasets/  
       • /home/user/datasets/raw/

    2. Files in /home/user/datasets/raw/ (initial mode 644, will be changed by agent)
       • data1.csv  
         ┌────────────────────────────────┐
         │id,value\n                      │
         │1,23\n                          │
         │2,\n                            │
         │3,45\n                          │
         └────────────────────────────────┘
       • data2.csv  
         ┌────────────────────────────────┐
         │id,value,score\n                │
         │1,10,0.5\n                      │
         │2,,0.7\n                        │
         │3,12,\n                         │
         └────────────────────────────────┘

    3. Nothing else exists under /home/user/datasets/.

    EXPECTED STATE AFTER SUCCESSFUL COMPLETION:

    1. Permissions
       • Directory /home/user/datasets/raw  -> 550  
       • File      /home/user/datasets/raw/data1.csv -> 440  
       • File      /home/user/datasets/raw/data2.csv -> 440  
       • Directory /home/user/datasets/clean          -> 750  
       • File      /home/user/datasets/clean/data1.csv -> 640  
       • File      /home/user/datasets/clean/data2.csv -> 640  

    2. Contents of cleaned CSVs
       • /home/user/datasets/clean/data1.csv  
         id,value\n
         1,23\n
         3,45\n

       • /home/user/datasets/clean/data2.csv  
         id,value,score\n
         1,10,0.5\n

    3. Log file
       • /home/user/clean_dataset.log (mode 644 is fine)  
         EXACT content (final newline **required**, no extra blank lines):

             data1.csv,640
             data2.csv,640

    The grading script will check:
       – directory and file existence  
       – octal permissions exactly as listed  
       – byte-for-byte match of the cleaned CSV contents  
       – byte-for-byte match of /home/user/clean_dataset.log
